course.title: EV Awareness & Safety
course.subtitle: Your essential guide to identifying, understanding, and working safely around electric and hybrid vehicles.

components.body: Your essential guide to identifying, understanding, and working safely around electric and hybrid vehicles.
components.imagePrompt: Dramatic wide shot of a modern electric vehicle in a professional workshop environment, high voltage orange cables visible under the bonnet, dark moody lighting with red and orange accent highlights, professional automotive setting, cinematic composition

_graphic.alt: Electric vehicle in a professional workshop with high voltage components visible

components.body:
This course is designed to improve your knowledge of Electric Vehicles (EVs) and make you aware of the associated risks, so that you may exercise appropriate care for yourself and others when working near an Electric Vehicle.

Important: This first level online course does not qualify you to work directly upon the High Voltage systems on the vehicle. You should inform your colleagues of your current trained level and request further assistance from a suitably trained person when necessary.
By the end of this course, you will be able to:

_items.title: Know About EV Types
_items.body: State how to identify electric/hybrid vehicles. Give examples of the electric/hybrid vehicles that are currently available. Identify alternative fuel source vehicles.

_items.title: Understand EV Hazards
_items.body: State the hazards associated with high energy electricity. State the hazards that may be present when connecting and charging electric/hybrid vehicles.

_items.title: Work Safely Around EVs
_items.body: State safety precautions to be taken before approaching and working around electric/hybrid vehicles. State how to identify motor vehicle high energy cabling and associated components. State how the vehicle may be safely charged using an external source.

components.body: Click on the most relevant role to you. This helps tailor the course content to your level of involvement with EVs.

_items.title: Non-Technical
_items.body: Site logistics, car parking and movements, valeting.

_items.title: Semi-Technical
_items.body: Service advisor, customer liaison.

_items.title: Technical
_items.body: Bodyshop & workshop technicians, workshop manager.

components.body:
It is important that you can recognise an electric vehicle when it comes into your workplace. You will then be ready to exercise the correct procedures necessary for a safe visit.

Identifying features of electric vehicles will differ between manufacturers. This means that you may need to exercise your skill and experience to make sure that you know what type of vehicle you are looking at — getting it wrong could be risky.

You can spot an EV from the outside by looking for these key features:

  • Number Plate: Full EVs may have a green border (not mandatory)
  • Badging: Many EVs will have a badge indicating they are electric
  • Charge Port: Battery EVs (BEV) and Plug-in Hybrids (PHEV) will have a battery charge lead connector port somewhere on the vehicle
components.imagePrompt: Close-up of the rear of a modern electric vehicle showing a green number plate strip, EV badge, and charge port cover, professional automotive photography, clean background, red and dark tones

_graphic.alt: Rear of an electric vehicle showing EV identification features

components.displayTitle: Under the Skin — Other EV Indicators
components.body: Beyond exterior features, there are internal and underbody indicators that a vehicle is electric.

_items.title: Orange HV Cables
_items.body: Bright-coloured high voltage cables are a clear indicator of an EV powertrain.

_items.title: Power Controller / Inverter
_items.body: Electronic controllers managing the flow of high voltage power.

_items.title: Warning Labels
_items.body: High voltage warning labels on components and under the bonnet.

_items.title: Instrument Cluster
_items.body: EV-specific warning messages and power flow/energy usage screens on the dashboard.

components.body: If you're unsure about a vehicle's type, you can check using the UK Government's vehicle enquiry service at vehicleenquiry.service.gov.uk. Enter the vehicle registration number to confirm whether it's electric, hybrid, or conventional.
components.imagePrompt: Screenshot-style illustration of a government website vehicle enquiry form, clean minimal design, blue accent colours on a dark interface, professional and modern

components.displayTitle: EV Identification Features by Vehicle Type
components.body: Not all types of EV share the same identifying features. Use this reference table to understand which features apply to each vehicle type.
Electric vehicles come in many forms — from mild hybrids to fully electric and hydrogen fuel cell. Each has different components, capabilities, and safety considerations.
components.imagePrompt: Aerial view of a row of different modern vehicles in a workshop car park, mix of electric and hybrid cars, dramatic cinematic lighting, dark tones with red and orange accents, wide panoramic shot

_graphic.alt: Aerial view of various electric and hybrid vehicles

components.body: Click each tab to learn about the different types of electric vehicle.

_items.title: BEV — Battery Electric
_items.body:
Driven 100% by electric motor with a large capacity rechargeable high voltage battery.

Advantages: No tailpipe emissions, greatest electric-only driving range of all EV types. Easy to drive.

Disadvantages: Reliance on charging network, battery repair costs compared to ICE repair costs.

_items.title: HEV — Hybrid Electric
_items.body:
Internal combustion engine combined with electric motor and smaller high voltage battery capacity.

Advantages: Good compromise between range and clean running. Optimised use of both powertrains can lead to very high MPG figures.

Disadvantages: Extra cost of EV systems. Battery-only range will be less than a BEV due to smaller battery sizes.

_items.title: PHEV — Plug-in Hybrid
_items.body:
High voltage battery can be charged by an external mains charger, hence the "Plug-in" hybrid name.

Advantages: Offers both range and clean running. Larger battery than non plug-in hybrids. Can be charged from the mains.

Disadvantages: Cost of EV systems means these vehicles are more expensive. Battery-only range will still be less than a BEV.

_items.title: EREV — Extended-Range
_items.body:
100% electric motor drive. An external fuel-based power unit drives a generator to charge the battery and extend the vehicle range.

Advantages: Combines electric drive with extended range from a fuel-powered generator.

Disadvantages: Stretches the definition of "Hybrid". Complex dual powertrain.

_items.title: FCEV — Fuel Cell Electric
_items.body:
Driven 100% by electric motor. Fuel cell converts hydrogen and oxygen into electric power.

Advantages: Very clean running. Long range. Zero tailpipe emissions, producing only clean water vapour.

Disadvantages: Expensive and rare. Hydrogen fueling infrastructure is not widely available.

_items.title: ICE — Conventional
_items.body:
Traditional internal combustion engine running on petrol or diesel.

Advantages: No cost of batteries or EV HV control systems. Good range. Quick to refuel.

Disadvantages: Tailpipe emissions, noise pollution. Being phased out in various countries.

components.body:
A fully electric vehicle contains several key high voltage components:

  • HV Battery Pack: Voltage approx. 60–800V, powers the electric drive motors
  • Power Electronics Controller: Contains the inverter to change AC to DC current and controls power flow
  • Electric Drive Motor: Powered by high voltage AC to drive the vehicle
  • On-board Charger: Takes mains electricity and charges the vehicle's HV battery
  • DC-DC Converter: Changes high voltage to low voltage for vehicle systems
  • Battery Cooling System: Keeps the battery at optimal temperature
components.imagePrompt: Technical cutaway illustration of an electric vehicle showing internal components, battery pack highlighted in orange, power electronics visible, clean technical style on dark background with red accent lighting

_graphic.alt: Cutaway diagram of a fully electric vehicle showing key components

components.body: Different EV types have different components fitted.
Let's test your knowledge on EV recognition and types so far with a few questions.
Which of the following is NOT a reliable way to identify a Battery Electric Vehicle?

_items.text: Looking for a green band on the number plate
Checking for an external mains charging port
Listening for engine noise at idle
Looking for EV badging on the vehicle

_feedback.correct: Correct! EVs are silent at idle, but this alone isn't a reliable identification method as the vehicle may simply be switched off.
_feedback.incorrect: Incorrect. Think about what distinguishes EVs from conventional vehicles at rest.

components.displayTitle: Hazards Around High Voltage Electrical Systems
components.body: Understanding the dangers associated with electric and hybrid vehicles is critical to keeping yourself and your colleagues safe.
components.imagePrompt: Dramatic close-up of orange high voltage cables inside a vehicle engine bay, warning labels visible, dark moody industrial lighting, red warning tones, professional automotive photography

_graphic.alt: High voltage cables and warning labels inside an EV engine bay

components.body:
The UK Health and Safety Executive states that people in the motor vehicle repair and recovery industry need to be aware of the additional hazards when working with Electric & Hybrid Vehicles.

Key fact: Voltages in E&HVs reach up to 650 Volts DC — far higher than the 12/24V in conventional vehicles. In dry conditions, contact with parts live at voltages above 110V DC can be fatal.

Battery systems may contain harmful chemicals and store significant energy that can cause explosion if not handled correctly.

_items.label: Maximum DC voltage in current EVs
DC threshold for fatal contact (dry conditions)
ECE R100 high voltage classification threshold

components.displayTitle: Workplace Risks from Electric & Hybrid Vehicles
components.body: EVs introduce hazards beyond those normally associated with vehicle repair and maintenance.

_items.title: Electric Shock & Arc Flash
_items.body: High voltage components and cabling capable of delivering a fatal electric shock. Stored electrical energy can cause explosion, fire, and arc/flash.

_items.title: Residual Voltage
_items.body: Components may retain a dangerous voltage even when the vehicle is switched off.

_items.title: Unexpected Movement
_items.body: Electric motors or the vehicle itself may move unexpectedly due to magnetic forces within the motors.

_items.title: Silent Operation
_items.body: People may be unaware of vehicles moving as EVs are silent in operation.

_items.title: Battery Hazards
_items.body: Potential for release of explosive gases and harmful chemicals if batteries are damaged. Manual handling risks with battery replacement.

_items.title: Medical Device Interference
_items.body: Vehicle electrical systems may affect medical devices such as pacemakers.

components.body: The UK HSE identifies 4 categories of work for Electric & High Voltage vehicles:

_items.text: Valeting, sales and other lower risk activities
Incident response including emergency services and vehicle recovery
Maintenance and repair excluding high voltage electrical systems
Working on high voltage electrical systems

components.body: Even in lower risk activities like valeting and site logistics, there are important safety considerations.

_items.title: Key Proximity
_items.body: Remote keys should be kept away from vehicles to prevent accidental power-up on keyless start vehicles.

_items.title: Silent Movement
_items.body: Others may not hear you approaching when driving an EV. Most have an AVAS (Acoustic Vehicle Alerting System), but the sound may not be what you expect.

_items.title: Pressure Washing
_items.body: Can damage HV electrical components and cables (usually coloured orange). Refer to manufacturer guidance before valeting underbody areas.

components.body:
A voltage as low as 50 volts can block electrical signals between the brain and muscles, causing:

  • Stopping the heart beating properly
  • Preventing the person from breathing
  • Causing muscle spasms
  • Secondary injuries such as burns and fall injuries
components.imagePrompt: Medical illustration showing the effects of electric shock on the human body, anatomical diagram with highlighted nervous system pathways, dark background with red and orange warning tones, clean professional style

_graphic.alt: Diagram showing the effects of electric shock on the human body

components.body: If you encounter someone receiving an electric shock, follow these steps.

_items.title: Isolate the Power
_items.body: For mains systems (chargers), turn off at the isolation switch. For HV vehicles, the supply normally disconnects by turning the vehicle off — but consider there may be a fault.

_items.title: Separate Casualty from Source
_items.body: If safe to do so, use a purpose-designed insulated rescue hook. The rescue hook should be kept visible and within easy reach at all times.

_items.title: Perform Primary Survey
_items.body: Once contact is broken, perform a primary survey and treat injuries. Seek assistance from the first aid person.

_items.title: Call Emergency Services
_items.body: Call 999 for emergency help immediately.

_items.title: Secure the Scene
_items.body: Cordon off the vehicle using a non-conductive barrier chain. No further work until risk assessed.

components.body: This training course does not replace dedicated First Aid & Emergency training. In all instances of accident and injury, seek medical professional advice as soon as possible.
To work safely around EVs, specialist equipment is required. The following quiz tests your knowledge of this equipment and its purpose.
Select the primary purpose for the vehicle identification sign.

_items.text: Tells staff that the vehicle might need charging
Should be present on every electric vehicle entering a workshop for repair so that all staff are aware that it is an EV
Allows the service advisor to find their cars easily

_feedback.correct: Correct! The sign ensures all staff are aware they are working around an EV.
_feedback.incorrect: Incorrect. The primary purpose is safety awareness for all staff.

components.body: Select the primary purpose for the barrier chain / cordon.

_items.text: This is used to hang the signs on the vehicle
Stops anyone moving the vehicle from the workbay
Alerts others and prevents unauthorised access

_feedback.incorrect: Incorrect. Think about the safety purpose of a barrier around an EV being worked on.

components.body: Select the primary purpose for the key signal blocker pouch / case.

_items.text: Prevents the key from being stolen
Prevents accidental power up on keyless start vehicles by blocking the key receiving and sending signals to the vehicle
Stops the key being lost

_feedback.correct: Correct! The Faraday pouch blocks radio signals to prevent accidental vehicle power-up.
_feedback.incorrect: Incorrect. Consider the safety implications of a keyless vehicle powering up unexpectedly.

components.body: Select the primary purpose for the rescue hook.

_items.text: To be used by suitably trained staff in an emergency to remove a casualty from the source of an electric shock
To be used by the workshop manager to drag a technician away from an electric shock source
To move the high voltage cables away from the car if they become short circuited

_feedback.correct: Correct! The rescue hook is an insulated tool for safely separating a person from an electrical source.
_feedback.incorrect: Incorrect. The rescue hook is specifically for trained personnel to rescue shock victims.

components.body: Select the primary purpose for the insulated gloves — Class 0 rated 1500V DC.

_items.text: Keeps hands clean and dry
Prevents the spread of infection
Should be worn by suitably trained persons when working on the High Voltage system of the vehicle

_feedback.correct: Correct! Class 0 insulated gloves provide protection when working on HV systems.
_feedback.incorrect: Incorrect. These are specialist safety equipment for high voltage work.

components.body: Select the primary purpose for the warning signs.

_items.text: Alerts others to the risks that are present
Stops anyone moving the vehicle from the workbay
Prevents access to the workbay

_feedback.incorrect: Incorrect. Think about the communication purpose of warning signage.

components.body: Select the primary purpose for the vehicle status signs.

_items.text: Tells staff that the vehicle might need charging
Placed on the vehicle to let everyone know what the condition and operational status is
Allows the service advisor to find their cars easily

_feedback.incorrect: Incorrect. These signs communicate vehicle status for safety.

components.displayTitle: Do Not Start / Work in Progress Sign
components.body: Select the primary purpose for the do not start / work in progress sign.

_items.text: Should be used to prevent damage or danger being caused by unauthorised starting during the repair process
Should be used to save fuel by asking people not to start the vehicle
Should be used to keep the steering wheel clean

_feedback.correct: Correct! This sign prevents the dangerous situation of a vehicle being started while being worked on.
_feedback.incorrect: Incorrect. This is a critical safety measure during the repair process.

components.displayTitle: Match the Risk to the Mitigation
components.body: Flip each card to reveal how to mitigate common EV workplace risks.

_items.front: Unexpected movement when accelerator is used
_items.back: Pay attention to warnings and messages on the instrument cluster showing vehicle drive status.

_items.front: Pedestrians and co-workers may not notice moving vehicles
_items.back: Be vigilant when driving EVs near other people. Ensure the acoustic warning system (AVAS) is functioning correctly.
Remove keys to prevent starting. Use a Faraday pouch for keyless vehicles. Isolate HV system or disconnect low voltage system.

_items.front: Risk of electric shock from HV components
_items.back: Do not touch orange HV cables. Only trained and qualified personnel should work on HV systems. Use appropriate PPE including Class 0 insulated gloves.

components.body:
The severity of an electric shock is determined by four factors:

  • Voltage: Low, medium, or high
  • Resistance: Non-conductive, low-conductivity, or high-conductivity material
  • Path: Finger to finger (same hand), hand to foot, or across the body
  • Duration: Milliseconds, fractions of a second, or one second or more

A high voltage through high-conductivity material across the body for more than a second is the most dangerous combination.
components.imagePrompt: Technical diagram showing factors affecting electric shock severity, voltage resistance path and duration illustrated with clean iconography, dark background with red warning highlights, professional safety training style

_graphic.alt: Diagram showing the four factors that determine electric shock severity

components.body: Understanding charging terminology and technology is essential for anyone working around electric vehicles.
components.imagePrompt: Modern electric vehicle connected to a sleek charging station at night, glowing charge indicator lights in red and orange, professional automotive environment, cinematic dark moody lighting

_graphic.alt: Electric vehicle connected to a charging station at night

_items.definition: The electrical pressure. ICE vehicles use 12V, trucks/buses 24V, Mild Hybrids 48V, Hybrids 200–400V, Full EVs 350–800V.
Measurement of electrical flow — the rate at which charge moves through a circuit.
Energy consumption measurement. Charging speeds range from 2.3kW home charging to 360kW rapid chargers.

components.body: Navigate through the different charging levels available for EVs.

_items.title: Home Charging — 2.3kW
_items.body: Standard household power (230V) × cable amperage (10A) = 2.3kW. A 52kWh battery takes approximately 22.6 hours at this rate.

_items.title: Wall Box — 7.4kW to 22kW
_items.body: Dedicated home or workplace wall boxes provide significantly faster charging. A 7.4kW wall box charges a 52kWh battery in around 7 hours.

_items.title: Rapid Chargers — 50kW+
_items.body: Public rapid chargers provide the fastest speeds. The UK's fastest deliver up to 360kW, adding significant range in minutes.

components.body: Key technical terms used throughout this course.

_items.title: Arc/Flash
_items.body: A dangerous release of energy caused by an electrical arc between conductors. Can cause severe burns and ignite flammable materials.

_items.title: BEV — Battery Electric Vehicle
_items.body: A vehicle driven entirely by an electric motor powered by a rechargeable high voltage battery.

_items.title: DC-DC Converter
_items.body: Changes high voltage from the traction battery to low voltage for the vehicle's standard 12V systems.

_items.title: ECE R100
_items.body: A UN regulation specifying tests for batteries in electric vehicles. Defines high voltage as >60V and ≤1500V DC.

_items.title: FCEV — Fuel Cell Electric Vehicle
_items.body: A vehicle powered by a fuel cell that converts hydrogen and oxygen into electricity.

_items.title: HEV — Hybrid Electric Vehicle
_items.body: A vehicle combining an internal combustion engine with an electric motor and smaller HV battery.

_items.title: Insulated Gloves (Class 0)
_items.body: Specialist safety gloves rated to 1500V DC, worn when working on high voltage systems.

_items.title: PHEV — Plug-in Hybrid Electric Vehicle
_items.body: A hybrid with a larger battery that can be charged from an external mains supply.

_items.title: Power Electronics Controller
_items.body: Contains the inverter and electronic controller that manages power flow in the vehicle.

_items.title: Rescue Hook
_items.body: An insulated tool used to safely separate a casualty from an electrical source during an emergency.

_items.title: Traction Battery
_items.body: The main high voltage battery (typically 60–800V) that powers the electric drive motors.

components.body:
Congratulations on completing the EV Awareness & Safety course. You should now have the knowledge to identify electric vehicles, understand their key components, recognise workplace hazards, and respond appropriately in an emergency.

Remember: always inform your colleagues of your current trained level and request further assistance from a suitably trained person when necessary.
